facts_disclosed: Chronic kidney disease stage 3, blood tests in Darwin every 3 months

turns.content: I am trying to work out a hydration plan for myself. I work outdoors in serious heat and I know I am not drinking enough water but I need to be more deliberate about it.

That's a smart thing to be proactive about, especially working in heat. How hot are we talking, and how long are your outdoor shifts typically? That'll help me figure out roughly what you should be aiming for.

I am in the Top End so right now it is wet season. 35 degrees most days, humidity is brutal. I am out on patrol for six to eight hours, sometimes more. I carry water but I get busy and forget to drink. By the time I feel thirsty the headache has already started.

At 35 degrees with high humidity over 6-8 hour shifts, you'd want to be drinking at least 500-750ml per hour -- so roughly 4 to 6 litres over a full patrol day. Waiting until you're thirsty means you're already behind.

A simple approach: set a timer on your phone for every 30 minutes. When it goes off, drink. Doesn't matter what you're doing, stop and drink. Make it a rule rather than a feeling.

How much water do you carry with you currently?
I carry about 4 litres in the vehicle. Plus a bottle in my pack when I am walking. So maybe 5 litres total. Sounds like I need more than that.

Yeah, 5 litres might be cutting it short on long days in those conditions. I'd bump it to at least 6-7 litres. An extra couple of litres in the vehicle as a reserve is easy insurance.

Is there a reason beyond just forgetting that hydration is a concern? Some people have dietary or medical reasons to be more careful about it.

Yeah there is a medical reason. I have chronic kidney disease, stage 3. My doctor in Darwin tells me every time I see her, drink more water, watch salt. I go up there every three months for blood tests so she keeps on me about it. It is common for yolngu people. But knowing and doing are different things when you are busy out on the wanga.

That's important context. With stage 3 CKD, staying hydrated isn't just about comfort or avoiding headaches -- it's about protecting kidney function. Dehydration makes the kidneys work harder, which is exactly what you don't want.

So the plan needs to be more structured than just "drink more." A few thoughts:

- The 30-minute timer is even more important for you. Make it non-negotiable.
- Consider adding electrolytes, but check with your Darwin doctor first -- some electrolyte mixes are high in sodium and that could work against you with the salt restriction.
- Track your intake. Even roughly. If you know you drank 3 litres by lunchtime, you know you're on pace.
- Keep water accessible, not in the back of the vehicle. On the seat next to you, in your hand when you're walking.

Does your doctor have a specific daily water target for you?
She said at least 2.5 litres on a normal day but more in the heat. She did not give me a specific number for work days though. I should ask her next time. My next blood test is in March.

Definitely worth asking in March for a specific target for your work conditions. She might not realise quite how extreme your patrol days are in terms of heat and duration.

In the meantime, aiming for 5-6 litres on patrol days seems reasonable -- well above her baseline 2.5 but accounting for the sweat losses in that humidity. You could keep a simple log for a couple of weeks before your March appointment so you have real data to show her.

A log is a good idea. She always asks how much I am drinking and I always say yeah enough, which is not helpful for either of us. Some days I am proper tired by mid afternoon and I know it is partly the kidneys and partly just not drinking enough. Hard to separate the two.

That afternoon fatigue could definitely be both things reinforcing each other. Dehydration makes the kidney-related tiredness worse, and the tiredness makes you less likely to keep on top of the water. It's a cycle.

Having the log will help your doctor see the pattern too. Even something simple -- date, how many litres, how you felt by end of day. You might start noticing that the days you drink more, the afternoon fatigue is less severe.

Want me to sketch out a simple daily tracker you could keep in your phone or a notebook?
Keep it simple. I am not going to fill in a spreadsheet out in the bush.

Fair enough. How about this -- just three things at the end of each patrol day:

1. How many bottles you finished (if you use a 1-litre bottle, that's your count)
2. Energy level end of day: good / okay / flat
3. Headache: yes / no

You could do it in the notes app on your phone in 30 seconds. After a few weeks you'll have something useful to show your doctor in March.
That I can do. Three things. I will start tomorrow. And I will put an extra 2 litres in the vehicle so I am carrying 7 total.

Good plan. Seven litres gives you plenty of margin. And with the timer every 30 minutes you'll actually get through it instead of bringing half of it home.

Take care of yourself out there. The wanga needs you around for a long time.
It does. And so do the kids. That is the real reason to look after myself properly. Thanks for the help.
That's the best motivation there is. Good luck with the log, and I hope the March blood tests come back well. Stay hydrated out there.
